title: Tunisian fish couscous
subtitle: Couscous with white fish, vegetables, and harissa broth
excerpt: White fish simmered in a spiced tomato broth with caraway, harissa, and vegetables, then ladled over buttered couscous. The Tunisian version of couscous (where harissa and caraway replace Moroccan saffron and ras el hanout), giving it a sharper, more intensely spiced character.
sourceNotes: Fish couscous is the coastal Tunisian preparation, associated with the fishing ports of Sfax and Monastir. The broth uses harissa and caraway (the characteristic Tunisian spice combination) rather than the saffron-based broth of the Moroccan version. Paula Wolfert and Claudia Roden both document regional variations.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The vegetable broth base can be made ahead. Add the fish to the broth only when ready to serve.

content.text: The fish is added to the broth for the last 10 minutes only — it needs no more than that and will fall apart if overcooked. Build the vegetable broth, reduce it, then add the fish for the final stage. Use a firm white fish: sea bass, sea bream, cod, or monkfish all work.

The broth should be assertively spiced and a deep red-orange colour from the harissa and tomatoes. Use the broth to hydrate the couscous, as with Moroccan couscous — the couscous absorbs the flavour of whatever liquid you use, and plain water produces a disappointing result.

items.prepNote: or sea bass, sea bream, or monkfish, cut into large chunks

content.text: Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a large pot over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}}, {{harissa}}, {{cumin-ground}}, and {{caraway-seeds}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}}, {{carrot}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 800 ml of cold water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes. Add the {{courgette}} and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{cod-fillet}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until just cooked through. Do not stir after adding the fish — break it into pieces only when serving.

Put the {{couscous}} in a large bowl. Ladle 500 ml of hot broth over it, stir briefly, cover, and leave for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork and work in the {{unsalted-butter}}. Mound the couscous on a large platter. Arrange the fish and vegetables on top. Ladle the remaining broth over everything. Serve extra harissa on the side.

items.cause: Added too early or simmered too vigorously
items.fix: Add the fish only in the final 8 to 10 minutes. The broth should be at a gentle simmer when the fish goes in — not boiling

items.cause: Harissa brand too mild or insufficient quantity
items.fix: Taste the broth before adding the fish and adjust harissa. Add more cumin and caraway if the spice character is flat

items.cause: Not enough broth used for hydration
items.fix: Use at least 500 ml of hot broth for 400 g of couscous. The couscous should feel moist and each grain should be separate after fluffing

content.text: Fish couscous is the couscous of the Tunisian coast, particularly the Sahel region around Sfax. While Moroccan couscous is defined by lamb, saffron, and sweet spicing, Tunisian couscous is defined by harissa, caraway, and the abundant fish of the Mediterranean. The two traditions share the same fundamental technique but produce a completely different flavour profile. This is a dish for a leisurely weekend lunch, assembled at the table and eaten communally.
